steps.title: Identify the evidence Brierley uses against Wright
steps.content: The key signal is Brierley's revised timeline: the humid period ended 500 years earlier than researchers previously calculated. This timing suggests that the drying began before Neolithic pastoralism could have been a likely cause.

steps.title: Apply the timing and eliminate the distractors
steps.content:
Brierley's argument is that the revised timing makes Wright's causal claim unlikely: the drying appears to have happened before Neolithic peoples could have significantly affected it.
  • Choice A — By pointing out that given the revised timeline for the end of the Sahara's humid period, the Neolithic peoples' mode of subsistence likely didn't cause the region's desertification: matches Brierley's timing-based conclusion exactly. ✓
  • Choice B — By claiming that pastoralism was only one of many behaviors the Neolithic peoples took part in that may have contributed to the Sahara's changing climate: misreads Brierley, who argues pastoralism likely did not cause the changes. ✗
  • Choice C — By insisting that pastoralism can have both beneficial and deleterious effects on a region's vegetation and climate: misreads Brierley, who argues pastoralism preserved vegetation. ✗
  • Choice D — By asserting that more research needs to be conducted into factors that likely contributed to the desertification of the Sahara region: misreads Brierley, who draws an inference from his model results rather than calling for more research. ✗
